words: And he said, what in the world is going on here?

words: I said, look right over there in the snake right there.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: You know how they are. When they first killed one of them and they first killed it. They killed it. It'll wiggle. They used to think they were doing quite a bit of that wiggling right down on the floor in the cabin. So that was about the best outfit. It tickled me.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: See that is dad jump on the gun.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, why didn't you tell me?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: I said, I didn't know he should be disturbing. I said, I want to be shooting. He said, you're going to get into trouble. I said, no, no.

words: I was about 14.

words: They had to sit down on the whole place. Yes. Yes, I know that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which place? Was this the place on the Palouse they tried to jump?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: They did jump. Oh, I thought... We left, we moved out in the neighbor's house. We had to give up a safe at a certain time. We moved out and went over in this garage. Them days they didn't have very much to move.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And the city of Amamoota was going in that house, and they couldn't do that. There was a lot of protection on that. So they just went over just a little bit over on the side there.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And they got them out. They just forced them out. A log, a rest of them.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And while they were out, the wind blowed their house and everything off down on the end of the road. That's why we told the very other kids, here too wind blows up.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: You see, we was little tired of it, you know?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And Dad told the man, I remember, my dad told her, you see that, you get them kids all in the house. Keep them there. You don't have to keep them anywhere, just in a while. Why up, because it'll all be over. It'll be good. One of them come back after that air tune, and they're going to come in to gate. Dad went into town the day before. And then the lawyer told him, the persecan's nine points in the law here. So he says, keep them out. Tell him to stay out. Just tell him twice.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And then he says, if we don't stay out, he says, shoot him. Ain't nobody going to bother you. And so he come up to the gate, rode up to the gate, and was going to give him. Dad says, that's it. Dad went up, and Dad had him, well, 45, 44, cold, the power. He had it in his hand. He said, you take another step, he said, in this way, or put the other foot through that fence. He says, I'll kill you. You stay out of this place. This is my land, not yours.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: They put it back up and went away. I heaped at Dad's head. Dad was taking pretty big chances. But he said, you stay away. Don't ever come back anymore. He says, I don't want to never see you again. Because he says, if you come back, you'll call me any trouble. He says, I'll kill you. He never came back. Then the other, there were three of them. The other two, up along the fence, they were going to build up on the ranch. They were going to another house and take this lumber. That is, they were going to build it over up there, see, and then fight it out in the law and court. So we just went up there, and Dad did, and Grandfather. He and Grandfather took a shotgun, and Dad took a revolver.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Grandfather's word was by Gaddy. He said, and he says, by Gaddy, he says, if you throw that plank, he says, over the fat fence. By Gaddy, he says, I'll kill you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As good as their word, is that what you said?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Yeah. He said something else, I ain't even going to tell you.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Because this thing will figure it out.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: There's pretty warm times here for a while. He's one of us kids, you see. You know, they could be killed, some of us kids. All of these, Grandfather took a shot, and got her antlers up, you see. Dad told him, he said, don't you cause me any more trouble. You've got me trouble enough now. He said, because if you do, he says, you won't live to tell the story. Dad was manly word to you. He sure, he sure can. And after we'd been, after we'd been over, and I was only about, I was about five years old, six years old. After we got over, we found out, who put him away, or who told him. He jumped to a bit of it, and always one of our neighbors, only a little mile and a half away, old man Hibbs. And he tied up a big ass, Hibbs, a Hindu, a Hindu. He told him that. He put him off, he put him right, so he could go about it that way. And they knew God well after his over with. That we'd find out, and appreciate that they stayed there, that something happened to him. There were two. Well, we'd killed him. You'd gone like, you didn't make me as mess with them in them days. You had done the right thing, or else you'd done, or else it wasn't, we wouldn't live to do anything more. That's what I was doing.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: If a man told you he'd do something, he'd do it. That was all there was to it, that's why there was no fooling.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And the boys, they didn't fight like places. You asked, you're not right. I had several flaps. And I was going to school. I'd have a captain every night on my way home to school.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: On two or three boys, they doubled teams on me. And I was pretty stoocumber. And I'd have to get a run and get out of the road. But if one of them boys didn't come to school, and the other one did, the one that come to school, boy he got a conference going home. I didn't get any of it. That's the way we were. Well, we just worked up and lived for ourselves.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: You had to. There were no officers and stuff around like ladies now. And you couldn't. If they did, it caused a lot of trouble. People took it in their own hands, boys. And people knew it, and they didn't do them things. That's what I was into it. They were good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever say anything to the Hibbs about what they'd done, what they tried to do?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Before. Told him, told him. Told him. He said, if you ever cross our path again, he said you won't live to see your family grow up. Now that's all he is to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager

speaker: Charles Crow
words: No, no, he stayed. He got out after this. After a few years,

speaker: Charles Crow
words: we made it to the hot farm, and they couldn't stay in the farm. They was pruning Hibbs and hiding their heads. And then they all go.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Hibbs, go to Hibbs place, go to that name yet. That land.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Yeah, he was quite an old man. He was a scoocomoo, but he was just about my height. But boy, he was tough. No, there was no. That was my mother's father. He was that tough old bird. And he's all logs. They'd haul wood down, logwood down in the timber. And he'd take them. He'd get a hold of the buddy end of the log and tell the others to get the other end.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: He was a scoocomoo boy. That's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say scoocom, what do you mean, scoocom?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Tough.

words: Did they try to jump other claims around the Palouse?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Uh -uh. They got out of there. They got out. They got out and left. They knew they wouldn't do the mistake. So everybody liked us. Because we were good neighbors everybody. And they stayed there. They had just been in trouble. Somebody killed them. Yeah. You know, when I was about 10 years old, I came to see a two -year -old heifer. Nice two -year -old heifer. Light in broad daylight. And went in and got it. In the past, he took it out and drove it off. And made a getaway with it. Then they never got him. They drove off. We saw him ever afterwards, a cow. And we got it back. But they never got him. He was riding a good horse and got out of there.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: All that, it was pretty tough.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Oh, you got used to it. He didn't notice it. But you had to get used to it. That's all there was to it. And our land was good. They were eating good crops. The way wheat, they made 30 bush

speaker: Charles Crow
words: and they went one day and took the wheat and come back home the next day. And took it down to the Snake River. Hauled it down there at the boat. They put it toward the boat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the Walley you took it to?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: The Walley. Yeah. The Walley. That's great.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: You know something, you've been around down there. I know a little bit of it. Yeah.

words: But I'd say three miles, a straight line. A merry whole line. About three miles.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Well, I'd still... I wish I'd stayed there. I wish I'd stayed there.

words: I got married down there, you know. I married a Colfax. I should have stayed down there. I'd been better off right into it. I stayed down there before I'd have formed. And I'd made money.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: I wanted a place of my own. I didn't have no horses or nothing. All I had was a driving team. So I didn't...

speaker: Charles Crow
words: I'd come up here.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: But... Oh, I've had a good life. Did you... I've been in Canada. I've been in Canada for 26 years. We had a farm up there. Trying to drive away the... to the south. We'd get no marshal, we'd get no crops.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And in one year, 12 months there, that we never got but about an inch of rain in the 12 months. All the marshals we got outside there was a little snow.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: That don't raise crops. No.

words: Bigger. Scatchewain.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And then there was... I liked that it was a big scope of land. Bigger was here. We were down here. We were down here about 8 and a 9 -10 miles. And here our ranch was. And then the other railroad was up here. It went through on about a mile and a half.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And so... and the road was running quite near a mile. It was always there.

words: Well, it wasn't my feet. It wasn't substantial. That out there was who we took a chance. If it didn't... If they didn't get a northern storm up out there, why you couldn't beat that for winter. Because they went down on the river. Down on the low parts. Where they kept them out there in the summertime. In the grass or something like that. Well, there had to be a lot of snow to fill that up. But they did fill up one more winter. It filled up and they... We... It's fifty miles there. About... Well, I'll say forty miles. I'm going to call it Cooley... Fifty miles. I'm going to call it Cooley Dam here. On down the river.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Yep. Wait a minute. Yeah. To where this place was.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And then... And it was Foster Creek. And there was a dam there now. Foster Creek Dam.

words: See?

words: On the corner of it. And what was the town that's near that...

words: They got a bad storm. We wasn't there that winter. And it was just the winter before we went out.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And... And we drove that fifty miles. Took that much seventy -five head of horses. That seventy miles. And all we seen was one... Iron Grey man pulled on. And she looked more like a... Reindeer than anything else. She pulled through the winter but she... She was running a pole and you couldn't really walk. And that's the only living heart that we seen. That wintered through on that matter. And I could show you where there was a hundred and fifty, two hundred head of horses. Dead horses. And rocks. Them big lone rocks. Haystack rocks, pilot rocks, and all them big rocks were over the prairie. That's where they consisted. The big rocks, the bunch of grass, up where...

speaker: Charles Crow
words: That was a real range. The best horse range I ever seen.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And now, I went over that... I don't know how many years ago. I guess it must have been about fifteen years ago. A million and another. We went across two years in the car. And they got a road ride right through there. And they got fences on that and they got a strange step. And they ain't no cattle coming in now. That's what they needed. That is to rent it. It's all owned. Keeps it open.

words: Did you just rent it? Huh? Did you rent the land? Out there?

words: Yeah. We didn't have to.

words: You didn't have to rent it or nothing?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: No.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: It was about seventy -five miles east and west. About fifty, sixty miles south and south.

words: That was all that bird's quest, right?

words: It was just a bunch of grass and stuff and some rock. But there was one butte there. And that butte wasn't really sad yet. And it had rocks around rocks on the up on the tire.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Doesn't want to go on sometimes. That you pick up anything. Pretty good. Sometimes you pick up too much. Well, I want to ask you more about these horses in the horse ranch.

words: I want to know, where did those horses go? Who did you sell them to?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Well, they sold them a different one. People bought to them and father sold them.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: He didn't get enough for them, but he got rid of them. And then we just farmed them back on the ranch and farmed them. And then we rented eighty, I guess it was eighty, forty. We had about 120 acres down there in the mountain. And we had about 100 acres of road or better. So it made over 200 acres. And we farmed them. You wouldn't know there were any buildings there though, now. They tore them down and turned away with them. And as good an orchard as it was in the country down there. We had all kinds of aquards. And we had parries. And plums.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And cherries. We had pie cherries that I didn't ever see anymore. And we had an orchard down the orchard. But when we left the ranch, we left it. And the parties had got it. It didn't have no use for it. And they died.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: My mother's sister, her brother, I bought the place. And they had a debility. So I just tore these down, grabbed out the orchards, and put them all to wheat. All this kind. Well, I guess maybe they done the right thing. I don't know. Because wheat's been a pretty good price ever since. Yes, that is decent. Dow is worth about better than $4 a bushel. And don't take many bushels.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Now you take an acre of ground. It was a good yield. And it was farmed right now. It'll yield 80 bushels to the acre. And now you get that out and $4 a bushel. That's money.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Well, that's what they're doing now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father take that place as a homestead? And he planted all those trees himself? The orchard? He planted the orchard?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: We planted everything.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Yeah, which is all this. There wasn't nothing. All there was was a house and a small barn. When we got it. I don't remember that much because I was too young. See, I was born in 8, and we come up there in 18.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: The folks come up there in 1860 something. I just don't know what the date was. But they'd come up there and grandfather had come up ahead of him. And he told them about it. And they bought that. They were $400. And they got that place. It was about a nice place. It was around there. So that's how we got there. And they went on rock that big on that place. All black soil. Wheat land, firm.

words: Had it ever been turned before?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: No, it never had been. It was all part of it. I had to break out 40 acres.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: After I got to the whole house. I don't know how old I was. I couldn't have been more than.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: 18, 19 years old. And when I broke up the last 40.

words: Was it easy to break?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: No, no, no, it was pretty tough. I had to have sunflowers. You know what they are. They put yards there. But, and they was about that. We were breaking the depth of the early end of spring. And we went over. I let them know about the higher. About so big. No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what do you think we've done?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: We just skipped. The flowers. Just cut off ground. Just over this corner back.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And this 40 acres belonged to my grandfather. And he was too old to break it up. We were breaking the depth of the early end. So I took the square. And took it over the house. And he had a fireplace. And we laid it down there on the fireplace. And we watched it. And watched it come to life. And you'd be a sunnage. You see the way they come out.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And it's burning.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And of course, we killed it. Didn't want it. They were lots of squirrels. But that's what he told you. That squirrel, he hibernated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He went to sleep, didn't he?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: He had a place built nest. And the flowers, the ears of the flower, just took off at the top.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And I happened to be right there. And I told the truth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dad said, right, guys?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: He said, that's right.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: The squirrel was dead. So I said, I know what baby is. I said, I'm going to take him over to the house and throw him out and see. I took him over there to Grandfather's house with his snow and laid him on his heart. He'd come alive. And we would run off. And it just stopped him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you were born on that place, huh?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: I was born on there at Grandfather's house. And that was just about a quarter of a mile from Dad's house.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Dad's house back this year. Grandfather went down the lane one night a little way here. Out here was a road.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Yeah, that thing.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And then Grandfather, anyway, they already just put a little way. A quick run down to the end. There was a bridge right there. And Grandfather lived there. We lived over here.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: We had a nice place in there. All that land in there, wonderful land. There ain't no rock.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: I saw a little rock. Some of it was a little hilly, but not bad. Dad's place didn't have a hill on it.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Oh, no.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Oh, no. But they're all in our slopes.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: But it's really, really weak.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: But it's a good farm by 160 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any bad years in the early years when you were there when they had real tough bad weather or bad harvests?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Yeah, in 93.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had really?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Yeah, I was. In 93, yeah. We sold our wheat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did we got thrashed?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: We sold it to the coast. We sold it by the carload, hauled it in town and shipped it to the coast. To them chicken treasures. And then we get to take it in groceries.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: In 93, in 94, I was wheat. They got it that way. In 93, nobody harvested anything out of there. I remember my wife's father. He had a header or a binder. And he got a green cut ahead of the others. And it turned out in the ice a lot. It dried up and they harvested. And he got that. He saved his crop. He didn't lose it. Well, we bought three wheat. They're explained from here. I mean, it's more of a lie.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: That's the way we got by, I think. But we didn't. I think he had 200 sacks in that sack. He had two booklets in each sack. No, I wanted two booklets. So that'd be 400 booklets. He had all that. And then we cut it with a header and he had it and stacked it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many acres were you farming then, in 93?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: In 93. We were farming around 100 acres and a hundred book better. We didn't have all the base no kept quite.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And so, everybody had lots of them. They had all the feed they wanted. Because a lot of them didn't get thrice at all. And that stock could eat that head of wheat. And it got good feed, pretty good feed. And they had any cattle. And they got by with it and stuff like that. But it was a trying time. Now, I ain't kidding you bit. Your whole year is worth. All just done went to pieces. Just rained and rained and rained and kept it in. My father's dad had a thrice at the thrice house. He had an engine and a face. And he started raining that night and it never quit.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And he quit raining. And he got it pulled out. Dad told him he said, it ain't no use. So he said, Joe Umpress was part of the neighborhood that was here. And he started to pull out. He just bogged right down. And then he couldn't, he couldn't. He wouldn't, he had to take horses and pull his machine out of there.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: I remember that. So it was tough. It was a boring holiday at this time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many families quit and leave then? Did many families leave the homestones?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: I couldn't. I couldn't leave. They didn't have nothing to leave with. They had to stay. They had this wheat, added wheat and stuff. And it was, it wouldn't do anything. It was nice that they couldn't sell it. And if they did like it, it made it just a sweet pea chicken, pea and stuff. And we shipped that, we sold it for a bunch of hours. We didn't need it. And we sold it and hauled it in and shipped it. And they got chicken feet, got a little money out of it, and got groceries. That's the way we get to you. That's right. We had a hard time of it. Now they ain't no foolin'. All your work is gone. And that's the only year of failure that I know of the police community. And then there was one year that the cops only went made about 12 visits to the Haker. I forget what happened that year. 12, 15 visits. But they got enough to live. That happened. They had quite a few acres.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And I broke out and they got by. Then after that they watched the carpool.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And then the man grew up, the price grew up on it. And you could buy for $25 a acre. You could buy that good wheat land. I could have bought 80 acres there in one spot there, right close for $17 a acre.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was when you were grown up? That was when you were of age you could have bought?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: No, no. I was only about 16.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Well, what I'd like to know more about is what that drive was like across from the Palouse over to your horse pasture country. What was it like driving the... How long did it take to drive the... Wait a minute. We make about 25 miles. 25 to 30 miles a day. And it's 175 miles. I like how long it takes to make it. It'd be about 7 days. It took a whole week, a little better. It took more than that because we stopped and I started to start feeding. I had some big grass and stuff. We had about 75 head horses.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: We had a night rider, a night herd. Dad took one part of the night, the after part. And these other boys took the other part. Two of them. And they come in about 11 o 'clock.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: And they said that they were two fellas trying to steal the horses. We got out there just in time. My dad had one of these old 50 -90s. He was here to see one of them, an army gun. And he had one of them. He got out there and it was a ridge.

speaker: Charles Crow
words: That distance must hold this ridge. It must have been pretty well onto a half a mile. And Dad did pull down with that big army gun and let it drive him. That boy, we didn't see any more of them. I don't know whether he killed one of them or not. But we never seen any more riders. And that was in the ground there where they abided. Well, you wouldn't know. Springs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it called?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Springs. Yeah, a lot of springs in there together. Every spring country. And we got in there. And they said that they had never been a hurry. Went through there, but what they lost, they lost pretty near half of their hurry. And five fellas who would steal them. They would run them off at night and then ride them up there and hide them in a contextual monkey around there. And they had lined up several faces like that going down there and up here. And they'd run them across the border.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Shall we?

speaker: Charles Crow
words: Shall.

speaker: Charles Crow
